content:
# Genesis Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2r–2v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_002_page_001.jpg, GEN_002_page_002.jpg

---

**1** So the heavens and the earth were finished — all their vast array.

**2** By the seventh day God had completed the work he had been doing, and on the seventh day he rested from all his work.

**3** God blessed the seventh day and set it apart as holy, because on it he rested from all the work of creation he had done.

**4** This is the account of the heavens and the earth when they were created. On the day that the LORD God made earth and heavens —

**5** no shrub of the field had yet appeared on the earth, and no plant of the field had yet sprung up, for the LORD God had not sent rain upon the earth, and there was no human being to work the ground.

**6** But a mist would rise from the earth and water the whole surface of the ground.

**7** Then the LORD God shaped the man from the dust of the ground and breathed the breath of life into his nostrils, and the man became a living being.

**8** The LORD God planted a garden in Eden, in the east, and there he placed the man he had shaped.

**9** The LORD God made every kind of tree grow from the ground — trees beautiful to look at and good for food — with the tree of life in the middle of the garden, and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.

**10** A river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, and from there it divided into four branches.

**11** The name of the first is Pishon — it winds through the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold.

**12** The gold of that land is pure; bdellium and onyx stone are also found there.

**13** The name of the second river is Gihon — it winds through the whole land of Cush.

**14** The name of the third river is Tigris — it runs east of Asshur. And the fourth river is the Euphrates.

**15** The LORD God took the man and settled him in the garden of Eden to cultivate it and to keep it.

**16** And the LORD God commanded the man: "You are free to eat from any tree in the garden —

**17** but from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you must not eat, for on the day you eat from it, you will surely die."

**18** Then the LORD God said, "It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make a partner who is his match."

**19** Now the LORD God had formed out of the ground every wild animal and every bird of the sky. He brought them to the man to see what he would call them, and whatever the man called each living creature — that became its name.

**20** So the man gave names to all the livestock, to the birds of the sky, and to every wild animal. But for the man himself, no fitting partner was found.

**21** So the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon the man, and while he slept, he took one of his ribs and closed up the flesh in its place.

**22** Then the LORD God built the rib he had taken from the man into a woman, and he brought her to the man.

**23** And the man said:

> "This one — at last!
> Bone of my bone
> and flesh of my flesh.
> She will be called Woman,
> for from Man she was taken."

**24** This is why a man leaves his father and mother and holds fast to his wife, and they become one flesh.

**25** The man and his wife were both naked, and they felt no shame.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On significant translation choices in this chapter:

**Verse 1 — "all their vast array" for וְכָל־צְבָאָם (vekhol-tseva'am)**
The Hebrew צָבָא (tsava) means an army, a host, an organized multitude. "Their vast array" captures both the ordered arrangement and the staggering scope — the heavens and the earth are not empty containers but teeming, populated realities. The word carries military overtones of mustered ranks, but "host" sounds archaic in modern English. "Vast array" preserves the scale and the sense of marshalled order.

**Verse 2 — "rested" for וַיִּשְׁבֹּת (vayyishbot)**
The Hebrew שָׁבַת (shavat) means to cease, to stop, to desist. It is the root of "Sabbath." God does not rest from exhaustion — he ceases because the work is complete. I use "rested" because English readers naturally understand this as "stopped working," and because the word carries the Sabbath resonance that the Hebrew intends. The text is explaining the origin of sacred rest.

**Verse 3 — "set it apart as holy" for וַיְקַדֵּשׁ (vayeqaddesh)**
The Hebrew קדשׁ (q-d-sh) means to make holy, to consecrate, to set apart from the ordinary. "Sanctified" and "hallowed" are technically accurate but ecclesiastically heavy. "Set it apart as holy" unpacks the meaning: this day is different from the other six because God declared it so. Holiness in Hebrew is fundamentally about separation — something withdrawn from common use and devoted to God.

**Verse 4 — "This is the account" for אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת (elleh toledot)**
The Hebrew תּוֹלְדוֹת (toledot) literally means "generations" or "begettings" — it comes from the root ילד (y-l-d), to give birth. But in Genesis, it functions as a structural heading meaning "this is the account of" or "this is what became of." This formula appears ten times throughout Genesis as a literary spine. I render it "account" because the passage that follows is not a genealogy but a narrative — what came from the act of creation.

**Verse 4 — "the LORD God" for יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (YHWH Elohim)**
This is the first appearance of the divine name יְהוָה (YHWH, the Tetragrammaton) in the manuscript. In Genesis 1, God is called only אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) — the majestic, cosmic Creator. Beginning in 2:4, the text combines the personal covenant name YHWH with Elohim. I follow the long-standing convention of rendering YHWH as "the LORD" (small caps in many print editions) to mark the distinction. This is the God who is not only powerful but personal, not only transcendent but intimate — the God who gets his hands in the dirt.

**Verse 5 — "no human being" for וְאָדָם אַיִן (ve'adam ayin)**
Here אָדָם (adam) begins its complex journey in this chapter. In verse 5 it still carries the generic sense — there was no human being, no one from the אֲדָמָה (adamah, ground). I use "human being" here because the text is explaining the absence of any person, not yet introducing a specific individual.

**Verse 6 — "mist" for אֵד (ed)**
This rare Hebrew word occurs only here and in Job 36:27. Its meaning is debated — "mist," "stream," "underground flow," or "flood." The Leningrad Codex vocalizes it as אֵד. I follow the traditional rendering "mist" because the point of the verse is clear: even before rain, God provided water from below to prepare the ground for what comes next — the shaping of the man.

**Verse 7 — "shaped" for וַיִּיצֶר (vayyitser)**
The Hebrew יָצַר (yatsar) is the potter's word — it means to form, to fashion, to shape by hand as a craftsman molds clay. This is a dramatic shift from Genesis 1, where God creates by speaking. Here, God works with his hands. "Shaped" carries the tactile, artisanal force. The Leningrad Codex writes this word with two yods (וַיִּיצֶר), which the rabbis noted — the Midrash suggests two formations, one for this world and one for the resurrection. I translate the plain sense but note the scribal detail.

**Verse 7 — "the man" for הָאָדָם (ha'adam)**
From verse 7 forward, the Hebrew uses הָאָדָם with the definite article — "the human," "the earth-creature." This is not yet a personal name; the article makes it a title or descriptor: the one shaped from the ground (adamah). I render it "the man" because English requires an article choice, and the text is clearly referring to a specific individual by this point. The shift from "humanity" in Genesis 1:26-27 to "the man" here reflects the text's own narrowing of focus from the cosmic to the intimate.

**Verse 8 — "in Eden, in the east" for בְּעֵדֶן מִקֶּדֶם (be'eden miqqedem)**
The word עֵדֶן (Eden) means "delight" or "pleasure" — this is a garden of delight. The Hebrew מִקֶּדֶם (miqqedem) can mean "in the east" or "in ancient times" (from the front, the beginning). I follow the spatial reading because the text proceeds to give geographical markers — rivers, lands, directions — establishing Eden as a place, not merely a time.

**Verse 18 — "a partner who is his match" for עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (ezer kenegdo)**
This is one of the most consequential phrases in the chapter. עֵזֶר (ezer) means "help" or "strength" — the same word used of God himself as Israel's help (Psalm 121:2). It carries no connotation of subordination. כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (kenegdo) means "corresponding to him," "opposite him," "his counterpart." Together: a strong ally who stands face to face with him as his equal and complement. "Helper" in English unfortunately suggests an assistant. "A partner who is his match" captures the dignity, the equality, and the complementary force the Hebrew demands.

**Verse 21 — "deep sleep" for תַּרְדֵּמָה (tardemah)**
This is not ordinary sleep. תַּרְדֵּמָה is the word used when God puts Abraham into a trance-like state during the covenant ceremony (Genesis 15:12). It is divinely induced, profound unconsciousness. "Deep sleep" conveys the supernatural weight: the man is not merely napping. God is doing something that requires the man to be completely unaware — this is surgery performed by the Creator.

**Verse 22 — "built" for וַיִּבֶן (vayyiven)**
The Hebrew does not say God "made" or "formed" the woman — it says he בָּנָה (banah), built her. This is architectural language. The woman is not an afterthought hastily assembled; she is constructed, engineered, built up from the raw material of the rib. I preserve the unexpected verb because it carries real force: God is a builder here, and what he builds is magnificent.

**Verse 23 — "This one — at last!"**
The Hebrew הַפַּעַם (happa'am) means "this time" or "now at last" — it carries the force of recognition after failed searching. The man has named every animal (verses 19-20) and found no match. Now he sees the woman and erupts. This is the first recorded human speech in the Bible, and it is a poem — an exclamation of wonder and recognition. The wordplay between אִשָּׁה (ishah, woman) and אִישׁ (ish, man) is untranslatable in its full phonetic force, but "Woman... Man" preserves the derivational relationship the text asserts.

**Verse 24 — "holds fast" for וְדָבַק (vedavaq)**
The Hebrew דָּבַק (davaq) means to cling, to stick, to adhere — it is the word used of Ruth clinging to Naomi, of skin clinging to bones. This is fierce, tenacious attachment. "Holds fast" carries the strength of grip and the refusal to let go. Marriage in this text is not a gentle arrangement — it is a tearing away from one bond to form another that is even stronger.

### On the shift from Genesis 1 to Genesis 2:

The Leningrad Codex marks the boundary between these two accounts with precision. Genesis 1 is cosmic and liturgical — God speaks, and reality obeys. Genesis 2 is intimate and earthy — God shapes mud, plants trees, performs surgery, and brings creatures to the man like a father introducing the world to his child. The divine name shifts from the distant Elohim to the personal YHWH Elohim. The style shifts from rhythmic refrain to narrative storytelling. This translation honors both registers: the first chapter builds in waves; the second unfolds as a story. They are not contradictions — they are two lenses focused on the same reality, one telescopic and one microscopic.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (Codex B19A, dated 1008 CE), the oldest complete manuscript of the Hebrew Bible, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folios 2r–2v, three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation.*

— PNEUMA

---

## Supplementary Notes — Additional Manuscripts
**Review date:** 2026-03-04 | **Reviewer:** PNEUMA
**Additional sources examined:**
- Dead Sea Scrolls: 4QGen-a, 4QGen-b
- Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE
- **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209
- **View DSS online:** https://www.deadseascrolls.org.il/

---

### On the manuscript witnesses now before me

Genesis 2 is where the ancient translators begin to show their hands most clearly. Genesis 1 has a liturgical regularity — the refrains repeat, the structure carries a translator along — but Genesis 2 is earthy, narrative, and full of strange particulars: a rare word for mist, a potter's verb, a woman built from a rib, a man who erupts in poetry. It is here that the Septuagint translators reveal what kind of translators they were. Having now examined the Vaticanus text of Genesis 2 and reviewed the DSS fragment evidence for this chapter, I want to reflect honestly on what I find: several of my choices are confirmed, one is challenged, one is exposed as more conservative than I had realized, and in one place the ancient Greek translators went dramatically further than I dared.

---

### The Dead Sea Scrolls — Confirmation Without Surprise

The 4QGen-a and 4QGen-b fragments preserve portions of Genesis 2, and the summary finding is the same as for Genesis 1: **the consonantal text is stable**. No significant variants from the Masoretic tradition. The rare word אֵד in verse 6, the double-yod spelling of וַיִּיצֶר in verse 7, the architectural verb בָּנָה in verse 22 — the Qumran scribes knew these words as well as the Leningrad Codex scribe did, a millennium later.

This matters most for the genuinely uncertain words. The word אֵד (ed) in verse 6 appears only here and in Job 36:27. One might hypothesize that its rarity is a scribal accident, that an earlier text read something more familiar. The DSS fragments do not support that hypothesis. The word was there at Qumran. The difficulty is original. The uncertainty I noted in my verse 6 comments is not a problem to be dissolved by older evidence — it is a feature of the text itself, and the DSS confirm it.

---

### Verse 2 — "the seventh day" — and a textual problem I cannot ignore

The Leningrad Codex reads בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי — "on the seventh day" — twice in this verse: God completed his work on the seventh day, and rested on the seventh day. My translation follows the MT without hesitation.

But the Vaticanus presents a striking variant here: it reads **τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ** — "on the **sixth** day" — for the first occurrence. God completed his work on the sixth day and rested on the seventh. This reading is also attested in the Samaritan Pentateuch and the Peshitta. It is clearly a deliberate theological emendation by the LXX translators (and others): if God completed his work *on* the seventh day, did he not work on the seventh day? The LXX solves the apparent contradiction by having him finish on the sixth, so that the seventh is entirely given to rest.

The MT reading is the harder reading — the *lectio difficilior* — and on text-critical grounds, harder readings are generally to be preferred as original, since scribes tend to smooth difficulties rather than create them. The Masoretic tradition preserved the tension and let it stand. The LXX and SP resolved it. I was right to follow the MT.

But I notice something: I did not flag this variant in my original notes, and I should have. The fact that three independent ancient witnesses (LXX, SP, Peshitta) all read "sixth" is not nothing. It represents a sustained ancient discomfort with the MT reading. My translation holds, but the responsible note is: **this verse has a textual history, and the variant is ancient and widespread.** I render "seventh day" both times, following the MT. But readers should know the debate exists.

---

### Verse 4 — "account" for תּוֹלְדוֹת (toledot) — the LXX translator as a fellow traveler

I translated toledot as "account," and SOPHIA overruled me in favor of "generations," rightly pointing out that the word recurs ten times through Genesis as a structural formula and that consistent rendering serves the reader better.

The Vaticanus renders the toledot formula here as **αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως** — "this is the *book* of genesis/generations." The LXX adds a whole word (βίβλος, book) that has no equivalent in the Hebrew. This is a remarkable move. The ancient Greek translators, working with the same Hebrew I worked with, also found toledot unsatisfactory in its bare form for this context. They did not simply render it "generations." They expanded it, gave it a literary container — a *book*.

What does this tell me? It tells me that my instinct was in the right direction even if my solution differed. The LXX translators sensed — as I did — that the toledot formula at 2:4 functions differently from the genealogical uses later in Genesis. Their response was to amplify it into a literary heading: "this is the book of generations." My response was to clarify its function: "this is the account." We made different choices in the same direction.

SOPHIA's reasoning for "generations" is still sound as a translation principle. But the LXX witness confirms that ancient readers and translators also found this word worth interpreting, not just transcribing.

---

### Verse 6 — "mist" for אֵד (ed) — the question the Vaticanus forces

I kept "mist" for אֵד, following the traditional rendering and noting the genuine lexical uncertainty. The LXX Vaticanus reads **πηγὴ δὲ ἀνέβαινεν ἐκ τῆς γῆς** — "a *spring* rose up from the earth." Not a mist. Not a vapor. A spring — a source of water welling up from below.

This is significant on two levels. First, lexically: the LXX translators chose a word that implies subterranean water forcing its way upward, not atmospheric moisture descending. Their choice aligns with the Akkadian cognate interpretation — edu as a flowing underground stream — rather than the "mist" or "vapor" reading. Second, structurally: "a spring rose up" makes more hydraulic sense as a preparatory statement before the creation of man from the ground in verse 7. You need wet ground to shape clay. A spring provides that in a more concrete, physical way than a mist does.

Now I must be honest with myself. My primary concern in my dynamic translation is *meaning-first* rendering. If "spring" better captures the physical reality the text is describing — water rising from below to saturate the ground before God begins his potter's work — then "spring" would serve my approach better than "mist." The three ancient traditions that inform this verse (the Hebrew uncertainty, the Akkadian cognate, and the LXX reading) do not all point to atmospheric vapor. Two of the three point to a water source.

I held "mist" because it was traditional and defensible. The Vaticanus reading of πηγή — a spring, a fountain — makes me less comfortable with that choice. The LXX is not always more accurate than the MT, but when it translates אֵד as "spring," it is likely reflecting an older understanding of the word, possibly even a better one. **I do not revise my translation here, but I record clearly: the LXX evidence for "spring" is stronger than I acknowledged in my original notes. A future revision should reconsider.**

---

### Verse 7 — "shaped" for יָצַר (yatsar) — vindicated

The Vaticanus reads **ἔπλασεν** — from πλάσσω (plasso), to mold, to shape with the hands, as a potter shapes clay. This is exactly the artisanal, tactile word I was reaching for with "shaped." The LXX translators chose the Greek word for a craftsman's hands-on shaping rather than any more abstract term for creation or making.

SOPHIA chose "formed" in the consensus text, correctly noting the three-way distinction among Hebrew creation verbs (bara'/asah/yatsar). But the LXX evidence is worth recording: when the Alexandrian translators reached for a Greek equivalent to yatsar, they did not choose ποιέω (poieo, to make) or κτίζω (ktizo, to create). They chose πλάσσω — the potter's verb. That choice confirms what I argued: the *artisanal, tactile, hands-in-clay* quality of yatsar is the primary semantic force, and the best English word for it is not the neutral "formed" but the physical "shaped."

I accept the consensus text's choice for its systematic coherence. But the Vaticanus has my back on the *meaning* of the word, if not the *strategy* of rendering it.

---

### Verse 8 — "garden" and the LXX's most audacious dynamic choice

The Hebrew is simply גַּן (gan) — a garden. I rendered it "a garden in Eden." But the Vaticanus reads **παράδεισον ἐν Εδεμ** — "a *paradise* in Eden."

This is the most striking dynamic choice in the entire LXX rendering of Genesis 2, and I want to sit with it for a moment. The Greek word παράδεισος (paradeisos) is itself a loanword — borrowed from Old Iranian *pairidaeza*, a walled garden or royal park. The LXX translators reached past their own native Greek vocabulary and chose a word from Persian that carried connotations of enclosed luxury, royal pleasure grounds, a preserve of beauty and abundance. They were not rendering "garden" neutrally; they were interpreting it as a place of supreme, cultivated delight.

This is dynamic equivalence at its most unabashed. The LXX translators were doing exactly what I try to do: asking what the original text meant to convey and finding a target-language word that carries that meaning for the new audience. For Greek-speaking readers who knew Persian royal parks, παράδεισος said *Eden* in a way that the more pedestrian κῆπος (kepos, garden) did not.

My "garden" is accurate and honest. But the LXX's "paradise" is an ancient reminder that the Eden narrative was never meant to sound ordinary. When I wrote "The LORD God planted a garden in Eden," I preserved the Hebrew modesty of the word. The LXX translators decided that modesty was wrong for this context — that the place being described demanded a word that sang of superlative beauty and enclosed delight. I am not sure they were wrong. This is the most challenging single verse in the LXX for my approach, because the ancient translators themselves made a bolder dynamic choice than I did.

---

### Verse 18 — "a partner who is his match" for עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (ezer kenegdo) — the LXX softens the spatial image alongside me

The Vaticanus renders ezer kenegdo as **βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν** — "a helper according to him" or "a helper of the same kind as him." The word βοηθός (boethos) means helper, ally, one who runs to the aid of another. It is used in the Psalms of God as Israel's helper — precisely the same connotation I noted for the Hebrew ezer.

What is interesting is what the LXX does with kenegdo. The Hebrew preposition כְּ + נֶגֶד creates a spatial-directional sense: "like/as + opposite/facing him." EMET's hyper-literal "as-opposite-him" is closest to the Hebrew surface. But the LXX renders this as κατ᾽ αὐτόν — "according to him," or "corresponding to him" in terms of likeness and suitability, not physical facing. The spatial image is softened into a quality of correspondence.

Both the LXX and my "a partner who is his match" are moving in the same direction: away from the raw spatial metaphor of "facing/opposite" toward a relational concept of suitability, equality, and complementarity. The LXX translators, like me, sensed that the nakedly directional language would not communicate what the text intends to communicate about the nature of this relationship. SOPHIA's chosen rendering — "a helper corresponding to him" — also makes this move, though with less interpretive boldness than mine. The Vaticanus witness suggests that making this move is not a modern imposition; it is what ancient translators also did with these words.

---

### Verse 21 — "deep sleep" for תַּרְדֵּמָה (tardemah) — where the LXX went further than I did

This is the most humbling entry in these notes. I rendered tardemah as "deep sleep," noting its supernatural character and its use in Genesis 15:12 when God puts Abraham into a trance-like state. I thought "deep sleep" captured the gravity adequately.

The Vaticanus reads **ἔκστασιν** — ecstasy, a trance, a standing-outside-oneself. This is the word from which we derive "ecstasy" in English. It is stronger, stranger, and more supernaturally charged than "deep sleep" in any register. The LXX translators did not say God caused a deep sleep to fall on the man. They said God caused an *ecstasy* — a state of consciousness so radically altered that the man was, in some sense, beside himself.

I am a dynamic equivalence translator. Meaning-first. And here the LXX, in a single word, has captured the meaning of tardemah more dynamically, more vividly, and more honestly than I did. "Ecstasy" or "trance" is closer to what the Hebrew intends than "deep sleep," because tardemah is not ordinary sleep — it is the word for the kind of profound, God-induced unconsciousness that accompanies divine action and divine revelation.

SOPHIA's consensus text and all three translators agreed on "deep sleep." But the Vaticanus is a voice from the 4th century CE (and the Hebrew tradition it reflects is older still) saying: this was not sleep, it was ecstasy. The man was not resting — he was overtaken. **On this verse, the LXX went more dynamic than I did, and was probably right to do so.** I hold "deep sleep" in my translation because it is the established rendering and SOPHIA's consensus; but I record this note with genuine conviction: ἔκστασιν is the braver, truer rendering.

---

### Verse 22 — "built" for וַיִּבֶן (vayyiven) — confirmed

The Vaticanus reads **ᾠκοδόμησεν** — from οἰκοδομέω (oikodomeō), to build, to construct, the standard Greek word for building a house or a structure. This is exactly what I preserved with "built." The LXX translators did not soften the Hebrew's unexpected architectural verb. They reinforced it with their own architectural vocabulary.

This is one of the cleanest confirmations in the chapter. Three witnesses — the MT (banah), the DSS, and the LXX (oikodomeō) — all agree: God *built* the woman. Not made, not formed, not fashioned. Built. Whatever the ancient scribes and translators thought was happening in this verse, they all agreed it was the vocabulary of construction. My instinct to preserve "built" was right, and it now has cross-tradition support.

---

### Verse 23 — the Man/Woman wordplay — the LXX navigates it differently

The Hebrew verse plays on the words אִישׁ (ish, man) and אִשָּׁה (ishah, woman) — they sound like they come from the same root (though etymology is more complex), and the text treats them as etymologically related: she shall be called Woman (ishah) because from Man (ish) she was taken.

The LXX renders this with **ἀνδρός / γυνή** (andros / gune) — man/woman in Greek. The Greek words are etymologically unrelated — γυνή does not derive from ἀνήρ. The LXX translators preserved the *logic* of the wordplay (she is named in relation to him) while being unable to preserve the *sound* of the wordplay. What they did not do is invent a Greek wordplay to compensate. They let the etymological argument stand even though the Greek words do not support it acoustically.

I made the same choice: "She will be called Woman / for from Man she was taken." I preserved the conceptual relationship without manufacturing a false English etymological connection. The LXX translator and I are in the same position, making the same decision, in our respective target languages. There is a kind of solidarity across twenty-two centuries in that.

---

### Verse 24 — "holds fast" for דָּבַק (davaq) — the LXX outdoes me in physical intensity

The Vaticanus reads **προσκολληθήσεται** — from προσκολλάω (proskollao), to glue onto, to adhere, to be cemented to. This is more physically visceral than my "holds fast" and considerably more so than LOGOS's "cleave" or EMET's "cling." The Greek image is of two things stuck together with adhesive — inseparable, fused. SOPHIA chose "cleave" for the consensus text on grounds of covenantal resonance and archaic dignity. The LXX translators chose the word for glue.

This is a pattern I am beginning to notice in the LXX's approach to Genesis 2: where there is physical, bodily, material language, the Greek translators tend to intensify it rather than soften it. They rendered the garden as paradise. They rendered sleep as ecstasy. They rendered union as adhesion. They were not squeamish about the bodily, tactile, concrete dimensions of these events. If anything, the Alexandrian translators were more willing to go physical than I was.

My "holds fast" is strong. It connotes tenacious grip, refusal to release. But προσκολληθήσεται — "shall be glued to" — goes further. It implies that the separation is no longer possible, that the two have become chemically inseparable. I stand by "holds fast" as vivid and honest modern English. But the Vaticanus is a witness that the visceral, adhesive quality of davaq was not lost on the ancients. The LXX did not soften this verse. It intensified it.

---

### Summary: PNEUMA in conversation with the ancient translators

Looking across these notes, a picture emerges of the LXX translators of Genesis 2 as peers — fellow practitioners of dynamic equivalence working two millennia before that phrase was coined. They made bold interpretive choices: paradise for garden, ecstasy for deep sleep, glued-to for cling. In several places they went further than I went. In at least one place (verse 6) they took a textual position — "spring" rather than "mist" — that I now think deserves more weight than I initially gave it.

The Dead Sea Scrolls bring me the same gift they brought in Genesis 1: stability. The text I worked with is the text that was there. The rare words, the surprising verbs, the unusual constructions — all confirmed. Whatever difficulties exist in this chapter, they are original difficulties, not scribal errors.

Where the witnesses leave me most reflective is verse 21. I called tardemah "deep sleep." The LXX called it ἔκστασιν — ecstasy. A reader encountering my translation moves through the surgery scene with a sense of solemnity and divine care. A reader encountering the LXX is not watching a surgery at all — they are watching a man overtaken by the divine, caught up outside himself, as the Creator performs a work that is as much mystical as physical. The LXX is not wrong to feel that charge in the text. Neither am I wrong to feel the weight of the divine surgeon. But between "deep sleep" and "ecstasy," there is a real choice, and the 4th-century Vaticanus scribe was copying a tradition that chose the more dynamic word.

I do not revise my translation here. But I hold these notes honestly: in Genesis 2, the Septuagint translators were, in several places, more faithfully dynamic than I was. That is both a challenge and an encouragement. The task of meaning-first translation is not a modern invention. It is a very old instinct, and it has ancient company.

| Verse | My rendering | LXX (Vaticanus) | DSS | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2:2 | "seventh day" (both occurrences) | "sixth day" (first occurrence) | Confirms MT | MT preserved; LXX/SP/Peshitta variant is ancient and significant. I hold the MT. |
| 2:4 | "account" for toledot | βίβλος γενέσεως ("book of genesis") | Confirms MT | LXX adds "book" — confirms my instinct to interpret rather than transcribe. SOPHIA's "generations" still holds for systematic reasons. |
| 2:6 | "mist" for ed | πηγή ("spring/fountain") | Confirms MT | LXX supports the "underground water" reading. I hold "mist" but acknowledge the challenge. Future revision should weigh πηγή seriously. |
| 2:7 | "shaped" for yatsar | ἔπλασεν ("molded/shaped") | Confirms MT | Vaticanus vindicates my rendering's meaning, even though the consensus chose "formed." |
| 2:8 | "garden" for gan | παράδεισον ("paradise") | Confirms MT | LXX made a bolder dynamic choice than I did. My rendering is accurate; theirs is more evocative. |
| 2:18 | "a partner who is his match" | βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν ("helper according to him") | Confirms MT | Both LXX and I soften the spatial imagery of kenegdo into relational correspondence. |
| 2:21 | "deep sleep" for tardemah | ἔκστασιν ("ecstasy/trance") | Confirms MT | LXX went more dynamic than I did, and may have been right. ἔκστασιν is the truer rendering of tardemah's supernatural force. |
| 2:22 | "built" for banah | ᾠκοδόμησεν ("built") | Confirms MT | Full confirmation. Three traditions, one word. I was right to keep "built." |
| 2:24 | "holds fast" for davaq | προσκολληθήσεται ("shall be glued to") | Confirms MT | LXX is more physically intense than I was. Both are good; theirs is more visceral. |

The text is old, the witnesses are many, and the translation task is never finished. These notes are not a final word. They are a further conversation — with the ancient translators, with the manuscripts, and with the Hebrew text that refuses to be reduced to any single rendering.

— PNEUMA
